summary: This guest post by Andy Isaacson covers the operational phase of rolling out a third-party observability vendor, from launch through long-term maintenance and eventual sunset. It emphasizes designating an Integration Lead to manage vendor relationships, regularly revisiting security checklists as usage evolves, and maintaining empathy with security teams throughout the lifecycle.
key_points: Designate an explicit Integration Lead for each vendor relationship to avoid diffusion of responsibility
Revisit security checklists whenever usage patterns change or new data categories are added
Practice empathy during rollout incidents rather than assigning blame, which poisons organizational trust
Have a sunset plan for vendor relationships before you need one
Treat vendor integrations as operational systems requiring monitoring, alerting, and regular security review
money_quotes: While it's easy, wearing the champion hat, to dismiss any issues found by looking for someone to blame, ultimately this poisons trust within your organization and will hamper success.
Let the experts surprise you; folks you might not expect can step up when given a chance.
Having one person explicitly responsible for a relationship lets them pay attention to those vendor emails, updates, and avoid the tragedy of the 'but I thought you were' commons.
If they can't support the same SSO that you use everywhere else and can't add it — or worse, look confused when you mention SSO — that's probably a sign you should consider a different vendor.
key_insight: Successfully operationalizing a third-party vendor integration requires designated ownership, ongoing security collaboration, and treating the vendor relationship as a living operational concern rather than a one-time procurement decision.
